subject: Literacy / ELA
topic: Long Way Down graphic novel and novel-in-verse study
materials_control_note: Original Mr Friess classroom package built from uploaded resources as scope references. Do not reproduce vendor pages verbatim; use original prompts, page ranges, and classroom-aligned organizers.
grade_subject_fit: Fits Grade 8 ELA/Literacy by combining novel-in-verse craft study, graphic-novel visual literacy, character development, symbolism, discussion, and analytical response.
unit_context: A 15-session Long Way Down study using shared reading, audiobook, the graphic novel edition, and targeted rereading.
assignment_purpose: Students track how structure, verse, visuals, character encounters, symbols, and nonfiction connections develop ideas about grief, revenge, rules, choice, masculinity, and cycles of violence.
student_task_flow: Launch with genre and visual literacy noticing.
Track setting, rules, and symbols during the first reading arc.
Review symbolism, poetic devices, nonfiction connections, and discussion questions.
Plan and write a short final analytical response.
checkpoint_release_logic: Daily sheets are formative. The final response is released after the floor encounter notes, symbolism tracker, and discussion prep are complete enough to support evidence selection.
teacher_implementation_notes: Keep first-read pacing brisk; use the organizers for rereading and evidence capture.
Do not assign every extension. Use the current day's sheet plus one focused share-out.
Treat the ending as open to interpretation. Require evidence, not one official answer.
likely_misconceptions: Students may summarize the plot instead of explaining character impact.
Students may treat the ending as a hidden-answer puzzle.
Students may copy long quotes rather than using concise evidence notes.
Students may describe graphic panels without explaining what the visual choice adds.
pacing_shape: 15-session sequence with a full daily packet and individual daily sheets.
assessment_focus: Short analytical response using claim, two evidence notes, explanation, and connection to theme, structure, or character development.
final_evidence_target: A short analytical response supported by two evidence notes and a student self-assessment.
success_criteria: I can cite or reference relevant moments from the text without copying long passages.
I can explain how Reynolds' choices shape meaning, not just summarize plot.
I can track how each elevator-floor character affects Will's thinking.
I can connect symbols, structure, and themes across the whole novel.
I can prepare and write a clear final response with evidence and explanation.

supports_scaffolds.label: Page-range support
supports_scaffolds.support: Each day names a reading range or review focus so students know where to return.

supports_scaffolds.label: Table organizers
supports_scaffolds.support: Daily task sheets use tables and record fields to reduce blank-page pressure.

supports_scaffolds.label: Quote limits
supports_scaffolds.support: Prompts ask for page references and short evidence notes rather than long copied quotations.

supports_scaffolds.label: Choice in final response
supports_scaffolds.support: Students can choose from theme, character impact, ending, or author's choices.

differentiation_model.support_pathway: Use the daily organizers as short-note tools, allow partner rehearsal before writing, and accept page references plus paraphrased evidence notes.
differentiation_model.core_pathway: Complete daily evidence tracking, participate in discussion, and write one supported analytical response.
differentiation_model.extension_pathway: Compare verse, graphic novel visuals, and nonfiction connections; push toward theme development and author's craft.

lesson_overview.overview: This unit moves quickly through Long Way Down while returning to key scenes for close reading. Students first notice verse and graphic-novel structure, then track Will's elevator encounters, symbols, poetic devices, nonfiction connections, and post-reading interpretations.
lesson_overview.essential_question: How do structure, voice, visuals, and character encounters shape Will's choice?
integrity_checks: Select activities based on reading pace and class stamina.
Use uploaded resources as coverage references, not copied templates.

teacher_guide.big_idea: The unit should feel fast enough to preserve the pull of the novel, but structured enough that students can track craft, character impact, symbolism, and theme.
learning_goals: Students analyze how verse and visual structure affect pacing, emotion, and meaning.
Students track how each elevator encounter complicates Will's plan.
Students use symbolism and nonfiction connections to deepen theme thinking.
Students prepare a short final response with evidence and explanation.
materials: Long Way Down and/or graphic novel edition
teacher_notes: Read quickly first or use audio for momentum, then return to key pages for analysis.
Do not turn every prompt into a full paragraph.
Use floor organizers to help students actively listen or reread.
For sensitive content, keep discussion grounded in text, theme, and choice rather than personal disclosure.
look_fors: Student can explain how a character changes Will's thinking.
Student can connect a symbol or structure choice to theme.
Student can support an interpretation of the ending without claiming certainty.
Student can distinguish summary from analysis.

shared_display_deck.title: Long Way Down
shared_display_deck.subtitle: Graphic novel and novel-in-verse study
shared_display_deck.speaker_notes: Use as the opening display while students get materials.

shared_display_deck.title: Our question
bullets: How do structure, voice, visuals, and character encounters shape Will's choice?
What changes Will on the way down?
What evidence makes your interpretation believable?
shared_display_deck.speaker_notes: Frame the whole unit around choice, pressure, and evidence.

shared_display_deck.title: Daily routine
bullets: Read or reread the focus section.
shared_display_deck.speaker_notes: Keep this routine stable; the content changes by day.

shared_display_deck.title: Floor encounter tracker
bullets: How does this floor affect Will?

shared_display_deck.title: Graphic novel visual evidence
bullets: What do you notice in the panel or page?
How does the visual choice affect tone, pacing, or meaning?
shared_display_deck.speaker_notes: Use with graphic novel pages or projected examples.

shared_display_deck.title: Final response choices
shared_display_deck.speaker_notes: Students choose one prompt, then gather two evidence notes.

shared_display_deck.title: Before you write
bullets: Conclusion connects back to theme, structure, or character.
shared_display_deck.speaker_notes: Use this for Day 15 transition into independent writing.

task_sheet.title: Long Way Down Daily Study Packet
task_sheet.purpose_line: Use these daily pages to track evidence, character impact, craft, symbols, and final-response ideas.
instructions: Use short notes, page references, and your own words.
Do not copy long quotations. Capture the idea and page.
Each day should help prepare for discussion and the final response.

tasks.label: Day 1 / Notice-Wonder: Novels in Verse
tasks.prompt: Explore what makes a novel in verse different from a traditional novel. Focus on structure, white space, line breaks, pacing, and emotional effect.

render_hints.heading: Notice-Wonder: Novels in Verse
record_fields: What I wonder about why authors choose verse
One thing to watch while reading

tasks.label: Day 2 / Setting, Rules, and Pressure
tasks.prompt: Track how the opening creates setting, pressure, and a rule-bound world. Use page numbers and short notes.

tasks.label: Day 3 / Object as Symbol: The Middle Drawer
tasks.prompt: Analyze how an ordinary object becomes loaded with pressure, choice, and danger.

render_hints.heading: Object as Symbol: The Middle Drawer
table_rows: How it changes Will's next choice

tasks.label: Day 4 / Figurative Chain: Conflict and Beef
tasks.prompt: Map how Reynolds moves through a chain of images to show inherited conflict. Use sketches or short notes.

render_hints.heading: Figurative Chain
render_hints.help: Reading focus: pages 25-27 plus opening review.

tasks.label: Day 5 / Floor 7 Character Organizer: Buck
tasks.prompt: Track Buck's connection to Will, the question or challenge he brings, and how this floor changes Will's confidence.

tasks.label: Day 6 / Floor 6 Character Organizer: Dani
tasks.prompt: Track how Dani changes the emotional stakes and forces Will to think about innocence, fear, and possible consequences.

render_hints.heading: Floor 6: Dani
table_rows: Question Dani forces Will to face

tasks.label: Day 7 / Floor 5 Character Organizer: Uncle Mark
tasks.prompt: Analyze how Uncle Mark uses storytelling and staging to make Will imagine consequences instead of only repeating the Rules.

tasks.label: Day 8 / Floor 4 Character Organizer: Will's Father
tasks.prompt: Track how family loyalty, grief, fear, and revenge collide on this floor.

tasks.label: Day 9 / Floor 3 Connection Board: Frick
tasks.prompt: Map character connections and cause/effect. Record how choices connect across people.

tasks.label: Day 10 / Floor 2 and the Ending: Shawn
tasks.prompt: Analyze silence, tears, the final question, and what Will may decide after the elevator ride.

render_hints.heading: Floor 2 and the Ending
record_fields: What Will wants out of, literally and symbolically

tasks.label: Day 11 / Symbolism Tracker
tasks.prompt: Track major symbols across the novel. Choose the strongest evidence and explain what each symbol represents.

tasks.label: Day 12 / Anagrams, Imagery, Juxtaposition
tasks.prompt: Choose strong examples of Reynolds' craft and explain how each one adds meaning.

tasks.label: Day 13 / Nonfiction Pairing: Revenge, Crying, and the Rules
tasks.prompt: Connect one nonfiction idea to Long Way Down. Focus on how the article helps explain revenge, emotional suppression, or Will's decision.

render_hints.heading: Nonfiction Pairing
table_rows: Why holding feelings back can hurt

tasks.label: Day 14 / Discussion Prep: Ending, Verse, Theme, and Character Impact
tasks.prompt: Prepare for a structured discussion or station rotation. Use short notes first, then choose one idea to bring to the class.

tasks.label: Day 15 / Final Response Planner
tasks.prompt: Plan one short analytical response. Choose one prompt, gather two evidence notes, and explain why your thinking meets the target.

render_hints.heading: Final Response Planner
table_rows: Connection to theme or author's choice

success_criteria: I tracked evidence across the unit.
I explained character impact, not only plot.
I connected craft or symbol choices to meaning.

symbolism_tracker.title: Long Way Down Symbolism Tracker
symbolism_tracker.subtitle: Track symbols and theme connections across the novel
symbolism_tracker.prompt: Record page references, strongest evidence notes, and what each symbol may represent.
success_criteria: I included more than one symbol.
I explained what the symbol may represent.
I connected at least one symbol to a theme.

character_impact_notes.title: Elevator Character Impact Notes
character_impact_notes.subtitle: Review how each character changes Will's thinking
character_impact_notes.prompt: Use this as a station, jigsaw, or review sheet. Focus on what each character shows, asks, or forces Will to face.
success_criteria: I covered all six elevator characters.
I connected each character to Will's development.
I prepared at least one discussion point.

discussion_prep.title: Long Way Down Discussion Prep
discussion_prep.purpose_line: Prepare one strong idea for a seminar, station share, or speed discussion.
questions: What does Will decide at the end, and what evidence supports your interpretation?
Which character's ghost has the strongest impact on Will?
Why is the elevator an effective structure and symbol?
How does verse change the pacing, emotion, or meaning?
What theme about revenge, grief, rules, or choice is most developed?
evidence_prompts: Question I want to ask the class

final_response_sheet.title: Long Way Down Final Response
final_response_sheet.prompt: Choose one analytical prompt: Will's decision, theme development, character impact, or author's choices. Write a short response using two strong evidence notes and clear explanation.
directions: Use two evidence notes with page references.
Explain how the evidence supports your interpretation.
sentence_starters: A key moment that supports this is...
success_criteria: I used two relevant evidence notes.
I explained how the evidence supports my thinking.
I connected my answer to theme, structure, or character development.

rubric_sheet.title: Long Way Down Final Response Rubric
rubric_sheet.subtitle: BC proficiency scale: evidence, explanation, and insight
rubric_sheet.learning_target: I can write a clear interpretation of Long Way Down using evidence and explanation.

criteria.name: Claim / interpretation
criteria.emerging: Claim is unclear or mostly summary.
criteria.developing: Claim is present but needs more precision.
criteria.extending: Claim is precise, insightful, and debatable.

criteria.name: Evidence
criteria.developing: Evidence is general or partly relevant.
criteria.proficient: Evidence is relevant and supports the claim.
criteria.extending: Evidence is carefully chosen and strengthens the analysis.

criteria.name: Explanation
criteria.emerging: Explanation is unclear or repeats evidence.
criteria.developing: Explanation partially connects to the claim.
criteria.proficient: Explanation shows how evidence supports the claim.
criteria.extending: Explanation connects evidence to deeper theme, structure, or craft.

criteria.name: Clarity
criteria.extending: Response is polished, controlled, and purposeful.

answer_key.title: Teacher Notes and Possible Responses
answer_key.note: Use these as discussion supports, not a fixed answer key.
possible_themes: Cycles of violence are maintained by rules people inherit without questioning.
Grief can pressure people toward choices they would not make with time and perspective.
Silence can communicate fear, pain, guilt, love, or warning.
possible_character_impacts: Buck challenges Will's confidence and exposes the performance of toughness.
Dani forces Will to face innocence, consequences, and the possibility of missing.
Uncle Mark makes Will imagine the act and aftermath of revenge.
Will's father shows the cost of inherited revenge and family loyalty.
Frick complicates blame by showing fear and accidental harm.
Shawn's silence leaves Will with responsibility rather than an easy answer.
answer_key.teacher_caution: Avoid treating the ending as settled. Students should support interpretations, not hunt for one official answer.

sub_plan.title: Long Way Down Emergency Sub Plan
sub_plan.summary: Students complete a standalone quickwrite, character review, or symbolism tracker depending on current progress.
student_tasks: Read or reread the assigned page range.
Choose one discussion question and write a short response.
teacher_notes: No sensitive personal sharing is required.
Students may work silently or with one partner if class norms allow.

days.reading_focus: What do you notice about novels in verse?

days.day_label: Day 2: Opening setting and the Rules
